si.t: DRAFT: Digital Solutions Workstream: Strategic Innovation for Polio Eradication (date of last update: 2/2522026)

Solution Current Application
Applicability to Polio Eradication
Challenge Addressed
Solution Name
Country Setting
Implementing Bodies
Features, Evidence & Outcomes
Documented Barriers, risk mitigation
Cost
Scalability and Sustainability
Performance Indicators
Potential Application on Polio Eradication
Source/URL
Comments
Migratory, conflict-affected
Digital
Humanitarian
Operational evidence
NA
Identify displaced populations and stakeholders in humanitarian response in OBX country
Non-census populations identified;
Humanitarian Data Exchange: https://data.humdata.org/ Displacement Tracking Index: https://dtm.iom.int/
Remote settlers
Polio
Case study
Borno, Nigeria
CDC GRASP Team
NA
Geospatial Research, Analysis, and Services Program
Campaign effectiveness
All
Can be scaled for surveillance, OBR and M&E activities., and adopted for activities requiring geolocation data.
Completion rate (expected use/actual use). Number of surveys created and deployed.Number of data entries collected and uploaded.
https://www.kobotoolbox.org/
Population Enumeration
Somalia

Multi-floor buildings are a challenge in house to house vaccination during SIAs. More time and human resources are needed to effectively reach every house at each level. Improved targeting of high-rise buildings in district that consistenly fails LQAS
Can be scaled in microplanning and post-campaign monitoring by correctly identifying number and floor of urban inhabitants, thereby minimizing loss in reaching target population.
New geographies identified, New geographies reaches
Guides microplanning.
WorldPop

Modelled population estimates available on a 100m grid for the entire globe. Situational datasets, IOM counts of camp and community populations of IDPs, UNHCR and other NGO counts of refugee populations,
Polio
Borno, Nigeria
Starting point for needs analyses or intervention planning, able to verify national or local population estimates for plausibility, combines directly with GRID3 features.
Democratic Republic of Congo
GPS Sample
Rapidly enumerate, sample, and navigate back to households all in 1 field visit Critical app for public health gold standard sampling in surveys
Digital
Afghanistan
Sudan, Syria, Gaza, Ukraine, Nigeria, Pakistan
Nomadic
Participatory mapping with satellite imagery to identify border crossing
GIS-based household serology survey to quantify levels of serological protection against poliovirus types 1, 2, and 3
Geospatial Visualization: Cross-border nomadic movement

Movement patterns of nomads identified in Gedo region. Destinations: Dolo, Luuq, Beletawa, Garbahare, El Waq, Bardera, Afmadow, Kismayo, Balad, Mogadishu. Nomadic mobility in the Gedo region is highly interconnected, with strong internal movement and several dominant destination hubs—particularly Afmadow and northern Gedo districts such as Luuq and Dolo. The network suggests both localized circulation and broader regional connectivity, with certain districts functioning as key population aggregation points.
Draft Additional Solutions (source: GAVI Zero Dose Project)
Strengthened PHCU linkage model
Strengthening Health Center--Health Post linkage; refresher training for HEWs; headcount of under-two children through house-marking; referral paper system; scheduling of response vaccinations.
Non-digital
Zero-dose, Routine Immunization
Ethiopia
97% of never-vaccinated and 97% of defaulters received appropriate vaccination within one month.
Long distance to health facilities; population mobility; discrepancies between population estimates and actual counts.
Recommended for scale-up to other regions with high ZD/UI burden.
Children counted, % discrepancy from DHIS2 estimates, ZD/UI children vaccinated through response.
Improving identification of missed children for polio vaccination; strengthening PHC linkage for surveillance and response.
House-to-house headcount of under-two children
Household enumeration with house-marking to identify ZD and under-immunized children, followed by referral mechanism.
Identified 332 zero-dose and 355 under-immunized children in 12–23 months cohort; 24% unvaccinated in 0–11 months cohort.
Distance to facility; migration; inaccurate population projections.
High — methodology straightforward.
Number of ZD/UI identified; % vaccinated through response.
Robust tool for improving denominator accuracy for polio microplanning.
CHW home-visit identification & mobilization system
CHWs trained to conduct home visits, identify ZD/UI children, engage caregivers, supervise peers, and provide demand generation.
Routine Immunization, Zero-dose
Cameroon
National EPI (SW), DHS Kumba North, DHS Kumba South, US CDC consultant
Identified and vaccinated 3,172 ZD children (Penta-1) and 2,893 Penta-3; engaged 9,450+ homes.
Fear of vaccination; misinformation; lack of routine CHW deployment; insecurity.
High — recommended for national adoption; CHW system already present.
Homes visited; ZD children identified; Penta-1 / Penta-3 vaccinated.
CHWs can trace missed children for polio RI/SIA activities.
FARID (Far-Reaching Integrated Delivery) Health Camp Model
Integrated mobile health camps providing vaccination, ANC/PNC, nutrition screening, and PHC services in inaccessible districts.
Non-digital + GIS-enabled
Zero-dose, PHC, Immunization
CORE Group Partners Project (CGPP), BMGF
40,619 ZD under-5 children vaccinated; 65,182 maternal consultations; 105,770 nutrition consults.
Insecurity; lack of nearby health facilities; access negotiation challenges.
Reported as cost-efficient.
High — model designed for insecure, hard-to-reach areas.
Number of camps; children vaccinated; PHC services delivered.
Reaching inaccessible clusters with integrated immunization including polio.
GIS mapping for FARID targeting
GIS-based identification of villages eligible for FARID service delivery.
Zero-dose, PHC
CGPP
Enabled targeting of 826 villages.
High
Villages mapped, ZD children reached.
Helps identify inaccessible settlements for targeted polio delivery.
Cohort analysis of ZD and learning attainment
National longitudinal study assessing relationship between early childhood ZD status and pre-adolescent learning.
Non-digital (research)
Zero-dose evidence
India
Université de Montréal, LSHTM, Princeton, Muso Health, Harvard
Identified ZD children in low-SES villages at highest risk of poor learning outcomes.
Indirect — ZD insights support microplanning prioritization.
Small-area Bayesian modeling of under-vaccination
Use of four-level Bayesian models to identify state/district/cluster variation in ZD, BI, MR-1, MR-2.
Zero-dose analysis and microplanning
Université de Montréal, FLAME University, Korea University, Harvard
Identified major pockets of low immunity; cluster-level variation largest driver.
Provides analytic foundation for national planning.
Supports highly granular targeting of low-coverage clusters.
EPI Supervision App for Outreach Monitoring
Digital tool enabling vaccinators to submit locations, inputs availability, vaccination counts, ZD children reached, and other outreach data.
Afghanistan
National EPI Afghanistan, Acasus, Gavi
Enabled digital monitoring of 14,000 outreach activities quarterly; major increases in outreach visits and ZD children reached.
Being scaled nationally; expected to identify ~300,000 ZD children in one year.
# of outreach visits, # of priority communities reached, # of ZD children identified and vaccinated.
Strengthening digital microplanning and accountability for polio outreach.
Geospatial dashboards for identifying missed communities
Geographic analysis of outreach data to locate unvisited communities and clusters with high ZD concentrations.
Zero-dose, Microplanning
Acasus & NEPI Technical Teams
Enabled monthly course correction and targeted outreach.
National scale-up underway.
# of missed communities identified, # of revisits conducted.
Identifying unreached polio-risk clusters.
Outreach performance review routines
National & subnational review cycles for identifying bottlenecks in outreach and ensuring follow-up actions.
Ministry of Public Health Afghanistan, Acasus, Gavi
Increased outreach visits by 88%; increased priority community reach by 87%.
Financial and operational constraints.
Already being replicated nationally.
% outreach visits completed; % children vaccinated.
Supports iterative improvement for polio SIAs.
Digital mapping of hard-to-reach communities
Deployment of nurses to map settlements via GPS, validated with other geospatial data sources.
Zero-dose, Surveillance, Microplanning
Democratic Republic of Congo
Acasus Project Team, DRC EOC
2,700+ communities mapped (15% of total).
Insecurity and remoteness.
Yes — now expanded to three provinces.
# communities mapped; mapping completeness.
Mapping insecure areas for polio campaigns.
Tailored vaccination strategies for varied terrains
Using boats for riverine areas, NGO vaccinators for insecure zones, and special teams for remote communities.
Zero-dose, Immunization Delivery
DRC
Acasus, local NGOs, DRC Health Authorities
1,300+ communities reached; 20k+ ZD children vaccinated.
Security risks; logistical demands.
High when integrated with routine outreach.
Community reach rates, ZD vaccination numbers.
Applicable for reaching riverine and insecure polio clusters.
Geo-enabled vaccination monitoring system
Centralized dashboard integrating coverage, performance metrics, and map-based monitoring.
Monitoring & Evaluation
Acasus DRC Team
Enabled weekly course corrections; improved session tracking.
Weekly performance reviews, independent survey verification.
Real-time microplanning adjustments.
Political economy analysis of routine immunization
Qualitative multi-level assessment of funding flows, decision-making, policy formulation, and constraints in immunization delivery.
Health systems analysis
Armauer Hansen Research Institute, Save the Children, LSHTM
Identified systemic barriers and contextualized strategies for reaching ZD.
Resource gaps, weak microplans, lack of tailored strategies.
Provides national guidance; analytical framework replicable.
Improving governance for sustained polio program delivery.
Crisis-affected ZD identification framework
Mapping ZD populations based on accessibility (opposition areas, rural, nomadic) via reports and NGO networks.
Zero-dose Identification
Sudan
Ahfad University for Women, University of Sydney, LSHTM, Sudan MoH
Mapped ZD children across crisis areas using IRMMA framework.
Insecurity; staff shortages; funding issues.
Applicable across conflict-affected areas.
ZD identification accuracy.
Highly relevant for conflict-zone polio surveillance.
Co-delivery of immunization with health commodities
Using platforms for COVID-19 vaccines, mosquito-net distribution, etc., to reach ZD/UI children.
Service Delivery
Sudan Federal MoH, UNICEF, WHO, participating NGOs
Improved reach in crisis zones.
Caregiver avoidance; programmatic funding issues.
Moderate — dependent on campaign synchronization.
# children reached during co-delivery.
Integrates polio vaccination into multisector humanitarian delivery.
Gender-responsive peer-to-peer vaccination model
Selected male and female caregivers trained to engage peers using motivational interviewing to identify and refer ZD/UI children.
Demand Generation, Zero-dose
Uganda
Infectious Diseases Institute (Makerere University), Uganda MoH, CDC
16,342 children identified; 12,451 under-vaccinated; 210 ZD identified and all vaccinated.
Language issues; low literacy; accessibility challenges.
High — leverages existing community networks.
# peer educators trained; # children referred; % vaccinated.
Peer educators can trace polio-missed children.
Multi-Zero-Dose (MZD) Indicator
Indicator combining four missed services—facility delivery, postnatal care, DTP1, and Vitamin A supplementation—to identify households receiving no essential services across the MNCH continuum.
Non-digital (analytic framework)
Immunization Equity, MNCH
30 African countries
Johns Hopkins IVAC, Gavi Measurement Team
Identifies communities where ZD children overlap with deeper service deprivation; 49.8% of ZD children are MZD.
High—indicator applicable across DHS datasets.
Prevalence of MZD, overlap with ZD, community-level MZD distribution.
Identifying deeply unreached communities to prioritize polio surveillance and SIAs.
Geospatial mapping of ZD/MZD disparities
Mapping ZD and MZD at regional, community, and household levels to identify overlaps and conflict-area vulnerabilities.
Immunization Equity
31 African countries
IVAC, Gavi, Uppsala Conflict Data Program contributors
Conflict areas show 3.5× higher MZD and 2× higher ZD prevalence.
Conflict, mobility.
High—method uses existing DHS + conflict datasets.
ZD maps, MZD maps, conflict risk overlays.
Identifying inaccessible and conflict-affected areas missed by polio RI/SIAs.
Routine DTP1 data as proxy for MZD mapping
Use of DTP1 coverage (<70%) to rapidly identify communities where most MZD children reside.
Routine Immunization Monitoring
Multiple
Johns Hopkins IVAC
Captures 88.6% of MZD children in communities with ZD >30%.
Data quality variability.
High—leverages routine data already available.
DTP1 coverage; correlation with MZD prevalence.
Rapid targeting of polio microplans using DTP1 as a proxy.
FLW record–based zero-dose identification
Using ASHA/ANM digital and paper registers to longitudinally track pregnant mothers, births, and children to identify ZD/UI early.
Non-digital (paper + existing digital tools)
Identification, Routine Immunization
India (Uttar Pradesh)
India Health Action Trust (IHAT), University of Manitoba
87% of ZD children had contact with health system (ANC, delivery, or other vaccines), showing strong potential for FLW tracking.
Poor data quality, incomplete entries, lack of facilitation, insufficient time for FLWs.
High if data systems are optimized.
ZD prevalence captured; FLW reporting completeness.
FLWs could track zero-dose and missed polio cases in real time.
MNCH service contact screening for ZD identification
Using contact with ANC, institutional delivery, vaccination, or Vitamin A to detect ZD children who passed through the health system but missed DTP1.
Identification
India, Indonesia, Pakistan, Philippines, Myanmar, Nigeria, Angola, Ethiopia
India Health Action Trust, University of Manitoba
>90% of ZD in India/Pakistan/Indonesia had some health system contact—revealing huge untapped detection potential.
Limited integration across services; record fragmentation.
High across LMICs.
% ZD with prior MNCH contact.
Use MNCH platforms to identify missed polio-vaccinated children.
Logistic regression models for predicting ZD/dropout
Weighted logistic regression to identify predictors of ZD and dropout using NFHS data, with class weighting for minority-class sensitivity.
Data science, Predictive analytics
IIT Delhi, Université de Montréal
Improved accuracy for predicting rare ZD/dropout outcomes.
Low prevalence leading to model bias.
High for national/subnational analytics.
Sensitivity/specificity of predictions.
Predicting dropout from polio RI schedules.
Neural network ZD/dropout predictor
Two-layer neural network with L1/L2 regularization and focal loss to improve minority class prediction.
AI/ML for immunization
Better at identifying true dropouts than logistic regression.
Data complexity; computational needs.
High for state-specific microplanning.
Model accuracy; recall of ZD cases.
Predicting high-risk areas for polio vaccine dropout.
FASTR rapid-cycle phone survey for immunization readiness
Quarterly panel phone surveys of 656 health facilities to monitor immunization readiness, supply chain stability, shocks, and staffing.
Monitoring, Readiness Assessment
Madagascar
Madagascar MoPH, Global Financing Facility, Gavi
Identified major gaps—electricity, stockouts, training deficits; captured shock impacts in real time.
External shocks; resource shortages.
High — could be replicated in all PHCs.
% readiness score; % facilities shocked; availability of tracer vaccines.
Real-time readiness monitoring for polio SIAs during crises.
Transit Point Vaccination (TPV) Model
Vaccination of under-immunized and zero-dose children at high-traffic informal border crossing points along Somalia–Kenya and Somalia–Ethiopia borders. Includes screening and vaccination on the move.
Zero-dose, Humanitarian, Mobile Population Immunization
Somalia, Kenya, Ethiopia
3,949 children vaccinated; 1,365 (35%) were ZD; majority from mobile regions (Gedo, Lower Juba, Middle Juba).
Lack of health facilities; very distant facilities; non-operational facilities.
High — adaptable to other border zones.
# children screened/vaccinated; % ZD; geographic flows tracked.
High relevance for cross-border polio transmission corridors.
Care Group Mothers (CGM) Immunization Engagement Model
Refugee mothers organized into community-owned groups, sensitizing households in Somalia and Kenya; tracking ZD children; providing basic health awareness.
Demand Generation, Community Engagement
Somalia, Kenya
CGPP Somalia & Kenya Teams
660 neighborhood mothers sensitized; identified 863 ZD children in Somalia communities.
Lack of facility access, operational gaps.
High — community-led model.
# CGMs trained; # ZD identified; # referrals.
Ideal for detecting missed polio-dose children in mobile refugee settlements.
House-to-house registration of under-5 children by VHTs
VHTs conduct door-to-door enumeration of children under 5 to identify ZD/UI children, following the RE-AIM framework to evaluate reach, adoption, and implementation.
Identification, Community Health
IDRC, UNICEF Uganda, JSI, local VHT networks
Identified 591 ZD and 816 under-immunized children among 3,013 registered.
Limited training, insufficient funding, resistance from caregivers, lack of supervision, short timelines.
Moderate—requires facilitation, training, and supervision support.
# households visited; # ZD/UI identified; completeness of registry.
VHTs can systematically identify polio-missed children in underserved districts.
Decentralized Immunization Monitoring (DIM)
Tool integrating LQAS, BeSD, and community-based sampling to identify ZD/UI prevalence, understand social drivers, and prioritize low-performing sub-districts.
Monitoring, Social Behavior Insights
Nigeria
AFENET Nigeria, Kano SPHCDA, JSI, Gavi
ZD prevalence: 23%; identified priority wards failing all antigens; improved resource targeting.
Low caregiver trust; reliance on fixed sites; limited outreach.
High—model supports ZD Reduction Operational Plans (ZDROP).
% antigen coverage; ZD mapping; caregiver intent scores.
Enhances microplanning in polio-endemic northern Nigeria.
BeSD-based caregiver behavior analysis
Structured behavioral assessments covering trust, intent, perceived safety, and logistical barriers to vaccination.
Demand-side analysis
AFENET, AHBN
Found that 70% of caregivers had low intent to vaccinate; 46% had low trust in HCWs.
Misinformation, cultural norms, insecurity, cost of access.
High—BeSD used globally.
Caregiver intent; trust indicators.
Supports SIA risk communication strategies.
Antigen-based ward prioritization
Prioritizing sub-districts where children fail BCG/Penta/Measles coverage thresholds.
Microplanning
AFENET, Kano SPHCDA
Identified three subdistricts failing all antigens for focused intervention.
Resource constraints.
Ward-level antigen performance.
High relevance to identify high-risk polio clusters.
Mixed-methods barrier assessment for ZD communities
Household surveys, HF assessments, and stakeholder interviews to determine systemic, behavioral, and structural causes of ZD.
Identification, Equity, Health Systems
AFENET, AHBN, JSI, Gavi
Identified 105 ZD children; mapped inequity between rural and urban service delivery.
Facility understaffing, supply gaps, caregiver affordability issues, mistrust, long wait times.
High—methodology easy to replicate.
# ZD identified; facility readiness; caregiver perception metrics.
Supports identifying polio vaccine gaps in underserved states.
Quality of RI service delivery assessment
Comparing rural vs urban facilities for staffing, antigen availability, training, and session completion.
Facility Readiness
Found rural facilities have fewer sessions, fewer staff, higher dropout.
Inadequate infrastructure, insufficient training.
Session delivery rates; facility staffing levels.
Minimum readiness criteria for polio SIAs.
Clan-mediated access negotiation
Engaging clan elders and leaders to secure access to conflict-affected villages and support health camp operations.
Humanitarian access, Community Engagement
Save the Children Somalia, local clan leaders
Enabled access to 10 districts previously inaccessible.
Local power dynamics; insecurity.
High in similar clan-based contexts.
# villages accessed; security incidents prevented.
Negotiating access to polio-inaccessible areas.
Mobile Integrated Health Camps
396 mobile health camp sessions delivering vaccination, ANC/PNC, growth monitoring, SAM/MAM screening in conflict zones.
Save the Children International – Somalia, BMGF
67,133 zero-dose children vaccinated (6–23 months).
Insecurity; logistical constraints.
# camps delivered; # ZD vaccinated.
Delivery mechanism for OPV/IPV in hard-to-reach areas.
Integrated service provider training model
Training 132 immunization staff, 140 CHWs, 140 outpatient nurses, 128 nutrition workers on immunization, data management, emergency care, disease surveillance.
Health Workforce Strengthening
Save the Children Somalia, BMGF
Enabled continuity of comprehensive PHC services in high-risk areas.
Training capacity; turnover.
High.
# personnel trained; service uptake.
Supports integrated polio + PHC campaign delivery.
Shock-responsive health facility readiness monitoring
Incorporating data on external shocks (natural disasters, outbreaks) into quarterly phone-based assessments to understand impact on immunization service availability.
Monitoring & Readiness
Madagascar MoPH, GFF, Gavi
Nearly half of facilities reported shocks; shock-affected facilities had lower readiness, informing targeted PHC support.
Frequent climate shocks; limited electricity, staffing gaps.
High—replicable in other climate-vulnerable countries.
Shock prevalence; readiness scores; service disruption metrics.
Supports rapid assessments of cold chain and SIA readiness during emergencies.
Transport voucher model for improving vaccine uptake
Financial incentive for caregivers to cover transport costs (most reported <$2), enabling access to vaccination services in underserved areas.
Demand Generation
Nigeria (Kwara, Oyo States)
Corona Management Systems, Kwara & Oyo PHC Boards, Gavi
Addresses affordability barrier affecting ~60% of caregivers; supports caregivers without travel autonomy; decreases dropout risk.
Funding sustainability; gender norms limiting women's mobility.
Low per-beneficiary; cumulative costs scale with population size.
High — adaptable to other regions with transport barriers.
% caregivers attending sessions after receiving vouchers; reduction in reported transport barrier.
Use vouchers to reach chronic non-attenders in high-risk AFP zones.
Multi-channel immunization reminder system
Strengthened use of SMS reminders, phone calls, home visits, and immunization card prompts to improve vaccination timeliness.
Digital + Non-digital
Demand Generation, Continuity of Care
Corona Management Systems, Gavi
Reminder coverage: 65% in Kwara; 32% in Oyo (baseline); linked with higher completion rates.
Low literacy; network issues; limited administrative data.
Low per-message.
High — can be automated at scale.
SMS delivery rate; % caregivers reached; schedule adherence.
Critical for OPV/IPV dose completion and tracking children requiring SIAs.
Community Health Committees (CHCs) for immunization coordination
Formation of 30 CHCs by clan elders and leaders to organize, schedule, and manage mobile health camps across multiple conflict-affected villages.
Community Engagement, Zero-dose Outreach
Save the Children Somalia, clan elders, CHCs
Enabled coordinated vaccination delivery across 10 districts under non-state armed control.
Community fatigue; navigating clan politics; insecurity.
High in contexts with strong traditional governance.
# CHCs formed; # coordinated health camps; attendance.
Mobilizes community structures to negotiate access to polio-inaccessible areas.
Local NGO–led mobile health delivery teams
Two local CBOs recruited 38 mobile health teams for delivering immunization + PHC services in conflict zones.
PHC Delivery, Zero-dose Outreach
SCI Somalia, local CBOs, BMGF
Delivered services to 67,133 ZD children; 98,039 nutrition assessments.
Security risks, logistical delays.
High in humanitarian contexts with non-state actors.
# mobile teams; # beneficiaries reached.
Useful for polio outbreak response in inaccessible zones.
Electronic child tracking system (U5 registry)
VHWs electronically register every child for immunization tracking, birth certificates, nutrition services, and follow-up.
Identification, Tracking
Zimbabwe
UNICEF Zimbabwe, MoHCC Zimbabwe
Strengthened ability to track ZD children in urban, peri-urban, and rural settings.
Digital access inequities; device limitations.
High — supports national immunization strategy.
# children registered; follow-up rate.
Tracking polio-missed children across hard-to-reach settings.
Modified Tanahashi Model for ZD bottleneck analysis
Adaptation of the Tanahashi service coverage model to identify why ZD children are missed and co-design interventions.
Microplanning, Health Systems
UNICEF Zimbabwe, MoHCC, University partners
Identified clusters with highest ZD burden (urban poor, remote rural).
Data availability.
Bottleneck indicators; ZD concentration mapping.
Apply bottleneck framework to polio undervaccination zones.
Multidisciplinary integrated mobile clinics & "Super Sunday"
Monthly/quarterly outreach clinics integrating immunization, nutrition, PHC, and birth registration; "Super Sunday" for mass immunization events.
UNICEF Zimbabwe, MoHCC
Substantial increases in ZD identification and vaccination across 11 districts.
Logistics, workforce shortages.
High for large-scale catch-up efforts.
Outreach attendance; ZD vaccination rate.
Integrated polio + RI campaigns.
LQAS-based identification of high-ZD/UI clusters
Using lot quality assurance sampling with decision value thresholds to identify clusters with ≥10% ZD/UI children.
Bangladesh
icddr,b, Jhpiego, Gavi MEL Team
Found 32% ZD/UI; urban slums highest (59%).
Requires trained sampling teams.
Low-cost.
High in LMIC contexts.
# missed communities identified; ZD/UI prevalence.
Quick sampling for polio outbreak risk zones.
Geo-targeted hotspot identification for ZD/UI
Rapid assessment combining DHIS2 and survey data to pre-identify 30 high-risk areas (slums, haors, hills, coastal, char).
icddr,b, Jhpiego
Guided the LQAS process; confirmed almost all areas as missed communities.
Varied terrain and mobility patterns.
# hotspots identified; match validation via LQAS.
Identifies polio high-risk geographies to target SIAs.
Two-stage randomized cluster ZD/UI survey
WHO-recommended sampling to estimate ZD/UI prevalence across 12 high-burden sites.
icddr,b, Jhpiego, Gavi
Found 1.9% ZD, 11.6% UI, 13.5% combined.
Large sample size required (n=13,800).
Prevalence metrics; demographic correlates.
Surveys can detect polio-immunity gaps.
Logistic regression modelling for ZD/UI drivers
Multivariate analysis identifying effects of geography, wealth, maternal education, parity, and child sex.
Analytics
icddr,b Research Team
Found children in rural remote & slums are 2–3× more likely to be ZD; girls 1.4× more likely.
Adjusted Odds Ratios.
Supports risk modelling for polio immunity gaps.
Rapid community vaccination walkthrough
On-the-ground walk-through tool to plan catch-up strategies in insecure/humanitarian settings, replacing standard microplanning.
Microplanning, Humanitarian Immunization
CHAN, Gavi ZIP, World Vision RAISE Consortium
Enabled rapid identification and clustering of eligible children; suited to shifting humanitarian access.
High security risks; shifting populations.
High for humanitarian contexts.
# eligible children identified; speed of planning.
Useful for insecure northern Nigeria polio microplans.
Quick-In/Quick-Out vaccination
Cluster children through community leaders and vaccinate rapidly to minimize security risks.
Humanitarian Immunization
CHAN Nigeria, Gavi ZIP
Delivered >45,000 Penta1 doses to ZD children aged 1–2 years.
Security threats; logistical constraints.
High in fragile contexts.
# doses delivered; % catch-up.
Excellent for insecure polio reservoir areas.
Multi-sector service bundling with vaccination
Bundling vaccination services with WASH supplies, nutrition, and tree-planting to improve caregiver uptake.
Integrated PHC
CHAN, Gavi ZIP
Increased demand and acceptance in high-risk communities.
Logistics.
Uptake metrics; service bundling coverage.
Can increase uptake of polio vaccine among hesitant groups.
Climate shock–responsive immunization planning
Use of flood and weather forecasts (Somali Water & Land Information Management) to anticipate immunization disruptions and pre-plan resource allocation, access routes, and service continuity.
Emergency Preparedness, Zero-dose, Humanitarian Immunization
International Rescue Committee (IRC) REACH Program
Successfully anticipated 2023 floods; immunization activities resumed quickly; prevented service collapse in high-risk districts.
Funding gaps; severe flooding; lack of mosquito nets and essential medications.
High—applicable to climate-vulnerable regions.
Continuity of immunization during shocks; # disruptions prevented.
Anticipating climate disruptions that impede polio SIAs and RI.
Cold chain prepositioning and mobile immunization response
Prepositioning solar direct-drive refrigerators, essential supplies, and mobile clinics at high-elevation secure sites; rapid deployment during disasters with additional 4WD transport.
Cold Chain, Outbreak Readiness
IRC REACH, OCHA, WFP, Somali MoH
Enabled uninterrupted delivery of key antigens (Penta1, Penta3, MCV1/2) during severe flooding.
Extreme weather; competing humanitarian demands.
High — model replicable across climate-stressed regions.
Vaccine availability during floods; cold-chain uptime.
Ensures OPV/IPV availability during climate emergencies.
Humanitarian immunization access negotiation
Coordinated negotiation with UNISFA, local authorities, and security organs to create safe access windows for mobile vaccination in highly insecure zones like Abyei.
Humanitarian Access, Zero-dose
South Sudan
IRC South Sudan REACH Program, UNISFA
Enabled immunization in areas inaccessible to government health services.
Armed conflict; mobility constraints; border disputes.
High in similar conflict contexts.
# of access windows negotiated; # children vaccinated.
Essential for reaching chronically inaccessible polio reservoirs.
PDIA community-led immunization model
Local leaders identify key immunization barriers and drive context-specific solutions, enabling adaptation amid insecurity and mobility shifts.
Community Engagement
IRC REACH Program, community leaders
Supported sustained vaccination despite fluctuating conflict patterns.
Requires high community trust; volatile contexts.
High—PDIA works in varied fragile settings.
Locally initiated solutions; campaign completion rates.
Can localize strategies for polio SIAs in conflict-prone counties.
Extended-age immunization (0–5 years)
Special permission granted to vaccinate ZD/UI children up to age 5 (instead of 2); vaccinators trained and equipped.
Zero-dose Catch-up
IRC South Sudan, MoH South Sudan
>45,000 Penta1 delivered to children aged 1–5; 60–71% doses for >1-year-olds.
Vacillator hesitancy for older children; policy limits.
Moderate—requires government approval.
# Penta1 delivered to 1–5-year-olds.
Highly relevant—polio catch-up for older children is often needed.
Border post vaccination for migrant populations
Immunization posts set up at official and informal border points during peak migration (e.g., Khmer New Year), targeting ZD children in transit.
Mobile Population, Zero-dose
Cambodia
NIP Cambodia, Clinton Health Access Initiative (CHAI), Banteay Meanchey PHD
Vaccinated 57 children aged >1 year; 15 were ZD; delivered OPV, Penta, PCV, IPV, JE, MR.
Coordination across ministries; limited off-hours operation.
High across migrant corridors.
# children vaccinated; % ZD.
Important for containing cross-border polio transmission.
Inter-ministerial coordination for border immunization
Collaboration with Ministries of Interior & Finance to authorize vaccination posts at border sites, beyond health sector.
Governance & Coordination
NIP Cambodia + relevant ministries
Enabled rapid deployment of border vaccination posts.
Slow AOP approval; budget gaps.
High for cross-border strategies.
Coordination approvals; # operational border posts.
Model transferable to polio high-risk border zones.
Integrated PHC services at border vaccination sites
Nutrition, deworming, PHC consultations delivered alongside vaccines at border crossings.
Integrated Outreach
NIP Cambodia, CHAI
Increased acceptability and uptake; maximized limited contact time.
# PHC services delivered.
Classic integrated model for polio + routine immunization.
BMU-led identification & referral system
Training 84 BMU officials across 48 BMUs to refer unimmunized children to health facilities; integration with community health units.
Kenya
MOMENTUM (USAID), BMUs, Homa Bay County Health Department
Increased Penta1 and Penta3 coverage; reduced number of ZD children by half in Rachuonyo North.
Fishing communities have erratic presence and mobility.
High — model is community-rooted.
# BMUs trained; immunization referrals; coverage rates.
Reaching mobile fishing settlements along Lake Victoria critical for polio surveillance.
Integration of BMUs into UAG community health governance
Formal engagement of BMUs within existing UAGs to institutionalize immunization advocacy and support.
Governance, Community Systems
MOMENTUM, Homa Bay County Health Management Teams
Improved coordination between BMUs and health facilities; sustained engagement.
None specified.
Participation frequency; referral patterns.
Sustains long-term polio information channels in remote lake environments.
Joint Animal and Human Vaccination (JAHVax) Model
Combined delivery of livestock vaccines and childhood/maternal immunization to reach remote nomadic pastoralist communities, leveraging veterinary service platforms to expand human vaccine reach.
Integrated Outreach, Hard-to-Reach Populations
Chad, Somalia, Nigeria (Fulani), CAR
World Bank Group; University of Liverpool; authors of included studies
Increased immunization coverage in communities otherwise unreachable; high acceptance because livestock health is prioritized by pastoralists.
Need for joint planning; limited One Health capacity; logistical complexity.
Potentially cost-saving due to shared cold-chain and transport.
Promising but requires multisector coordination.
# joint sessions; human vaccination coverage; livestock vaccination coverage.
Mobile, cross-sector campaigns ideal for polio SIAs in nomadic belts.
Integrated human and animal health outreach (One Health service integration)
Adding PHC services (e.g., deworming, antenatal care) to joint vaccination days to increase uptake and social acceptability.
One Health, Community Engagement
Chad, Somalia, Nigeria
World Bank Group; field teams in Chad, Somalia, Nigeria
Enhanced participation due to alignment with community needs; addresses barriers to reaching ZD children.
Coordination challenges; limited funding.
Shared logistics reduce cost.
High in pastoralist regions.
Uptake of added PHC services; immunization coverage.
Supports mobile OPV/IPV delivery to migratory groups.
Embedded Implementation Research (IR) approach
Multi-stakeholder research integrated within immunization programs to diagnose bottlenecks and co-design interventions.
Policy, Program Design, Equity
Nigeria, India, Chad, Ethiopia, Kenya, Pakistan, Uganda
UNICEF HQ, WHO AHPSR, local research teams in 10 countries
63% of IR projects led to policy or program changes; improved routine coverage in multiple countries.
Insufficient political will; limited budget for implementation of findings.
Moderate; depends on research scale.
High — recommended globally.
Policy changes; implementation of IR recommendations; capacity-building metrics.
IR can identify polio surveillance bottlenecks and SIA effectiveness issues.
Immunization IR capacity-building model
Training national and subnational teams in IR methods, bottleneck analysis, and evidence application.
Workforce Strengthening
10 participating LMICs
UNICEF, WHO AHPSR
85% of trained personnel used IR skills elsewhere, increasing sustainability.
Limited follow-up support.
# trainees; evidence of practice in other programs.
Strengthens capacity for polio program evaluation.
Pro-equity intervention evidence mapping
Rapid evidence synthesis across IRMMA domains to classify interventions as "proven," "promising," "inconclusive," or "potentially ineffective."
Equity, Strategy Prioritization
Global
FHI 360 Global Programs & Science; Gavi MEL Team
Identified 1 proven (incentives), 9 promising interventions.
Evidence gaps in ZD-specific research.
High — used for global planning.
Classification outputs; evidence strength.
Guides selection of high-impact polio equity interventions.
Implementation determinant mapping (Proctor's Taxonomy)
Mapping acceptability, feasibility, adoption, cost, sustainability, fidelity, and penetration for equity interventions.
Implementation Science
FHI 360, Gavi
Identified consistent enablers (community ownership, coordination) and barriers (cost, logistics, data).
Weak data systems in some settings.
Implementation outcomes.
Supports SIA quality improvement.
IA2030 zero-dose living scoping review
Continuous systematic review updated annually to track global ZD evidence, barriers, and equity insights.
Evidence Synthesis
LMICs globally
ESPUM, CRCHUM, Gavi
Identified 82 studies; highlighted evidence gaps (no ZD-specific delivery interventions).
No standard ZD operational studies.
# studies added annually; thematic coverage.
Helps track evidence on polio zero-dose correlates and missed communities.
ZD deprivation mapping
Mapping educational, geographic, urban/rural, SES, and conflict-related disadvantages faced by ZD children.
Equity Analytics
Global LMICs
ESPUM, CRCHUM
Identified overlapping disadvantages driving ZD status.
Data completeness.
Deprivation indices.
Identifies polio "missed communities."
Human-Centered Design for immunization demand & service design
HCWs, parents of ZD children, and community leaders co-created solutions through participatory workshops.
Co-Design, Demand Generation
Women Advocates for Vaccine Access, DCL, Johns Hopkins IVAC
Produced tailored local strategies (mobile services, addressing male decision-making drivers).
Complex sociocultural drivers; security issues.
High — HCD transferable.
# HCD sessions; implemented action plans.
Designing targeted SIA messaging in high-refusal clusters.
Multidimensional ZD driver mapping
Categorizing ZD causes across service delivery, workforce, financing, supply chain, governance, and data systems.
Health Systems Strengthening
Women Advocates for Vaccine Access, DCL, IVAC
Identified workforce shortages, poor facility access, insecurity, mistrust, and cultural norms.
Resource gaps, insecurity, gender norms.
Systemic bottlenecks addressed.
Improves risk zoning for polio SIAs.
Tailored community-driven immunization messaging
Engaging traditional and religious leaders, addressing vaccine myths, improving interpersonal communication.
Improved acceptance in resistant communities.
Entrenched cultural beliefs.
Caregiver acceptance; community outreach sessions.
Reduces refusals in polio-endemic refusal clusters.
Humanitarian inter-agency referral network and updated migration data
Surveillance, OBR, network, analyticx

1) Interagency partnership with in-countryhumanitarian focal points referring U5 populations with needed services, 2) Sharing data across humanitarian organizations to identify U5 populations in H2R areas
Syria, Yemen, Horn of Africa, South Sudan, and the Democratic Republic of Congo
Migratory, conflict-affected, internally displaced populations
UN OCHA, WHO, IOM, Unicef, WFP

Better coordination, data sharing and resource allocation for targetted outreach in education, health, shelter, WASH, food security. While open source platforns such as Displacement Tracking Matrix and Humanitarian Data Exchange are publicly available, GPEI currently does not have unifrom access to real-time, province and border-level in children U5 eligible for immunization.
Data security and privacy concerns. Variability in data quality and completeness.
Potential Applicability to on Polio Eradication

Missing data retrieved from humanitarian partners can be directly incorporated in in-country microplanning and enhance denominator validity in routine polio surveillance. This model can be applied to both countries experiencing conflict or receiving displaced populations.
Mobility & Migration
Surveillamce, OBR, analytics
CDC-led case study triangulating multiple outreach data between 2016-2020. High-resolution imagery to assess habitation and destruction patterns.
Geospatial Tracking System: Satellite imagery analysis w/ vaccinator GPS tracking to identify settlement habitation

Identifies inhabited/uninhabited settlements combining satellite imagery, modeled settlement data, and population estimates with vaccinator GPS tracking to create a automated, unified view of both population distribution, settlement extents, building footprints, and campaign reach.
Provide high‑resolution satellite imagery to determine habitation and settlement status for microplanning in campaigns or outreach immunization.
Digital standardization of campaign forms using open-access tool.
umanitarian
Djibouti, Nigeria, Pakistan, Afghanistan, Ukraine, Egypt, Colombia, Kenya
UNHCR, UNICEF, IFRC, Oxfam, UN Women, IFRC
KoBoToolbox is a web-based and mobile application that allows users to create surveys, collect data offline, and upload it when an internet connection is available.

Used in intra-campaign polio OBR monitoring, outreach immunization services in Egypt, and other ddata collection activities in humanitarian settings. Can collect data offline, used mixed methods surveys, and generate automatic reports of survey results. Access can be managed by more than one user.It supports various data types, including text, images, and GPS coordinates, admin credentials can be tranferred and automated reports can be generated within app.
KoBoToolbox can be adapted for polio eradication efforts by creating specific surveys for polio surveillance, vaccination coverage, and outbreak response-include synchronized SIAs.
Training/orientation required for staff to use the platform effectively.
Can be done remotely (yes/no_
Yes
Yes (vaccinators in-country)
Yes (survey created virtually, access handed to EPI & GPEI coordinator)
OBR, governance and oversight, analytics
Geospatial Tracking System: Vaccine Buddy
Passive GPS tracking device inside vaccine carrier.
Effective tracking in insecure areas. Automatically collects: geolocation, temperature, lid opens/close
Previously unidentified settlements located.
Campaign geographic coverage (expected vs. actual)
Tracking vaccinator movement to ensure coverage verification and effective monitoring/accountability during campaign.
CDC Geospatial Research, Analysis, and Services Program
Can be used in other country settings with urban population concentrations.
Additional housing units identified per floor
Mapping high-rise buildings
Mapping high-rise building clusters to guide vaccination team deployment.
OBR, governance and oversight
Solution Name
OBR, governance and
Can provide baseline accounting of underimmunized (zero-dose, defaulters) and improve microplanning accuracy.

Automatically generated settlement extents and building footprints. GRID3 has algorithmically combined these footprints into “settlements” with GIS boundaries.Big data powerhouses like Google, Meta, Microsoft, and others have used massive computing resources to automatically scan satellite imagery and identify likely human-made structures.
Automatically generated settlement extents and building footprints provide a fast, reliable way to locate and quantify populations living in remote or poorly mapped areas.

Datasets are produced globally, allowing polio programs to triage settlements by estimated population size, accessibility, and distance from essential resources, and to apply the same data-driven approach across multiple geographies, including cross-border transmission zones.

Although often differing from the actual political boundaries of a given settlement on the ground, they offer a way to rapidly LOCATE, IDENTIFY, and QUANTIFY population in the most remote areas where traditional maps fall short. They can be used to rapidly triage and target areas for programmatic work by estimated population size, distance from resources, and other geographic factors.
Mapping, enumeration, microplanning, validation, team tracking, data integration-field staff combined with satellite imagery.

Engage local staff to map settlements, facilities, and boundaries using GPS and satellite imagery. Enumeration: GPS-tag house-to-house data on children (0–23 months), vaccination status, and non-vaccination reasons. Microplanning: Use maps showing never-vaccinated rates, main reasons, and facility locations to target high-risk areas. Validation: Clean and verify spatial data with local input, GRID3, and satellite imagery. Team Tracking: GPS-track vaccination teams to confirm locality visits and coverage of remote areas. Data Integration: Link enumeration with boundaries to estimate missed children and visualize coverage post-campaign.

Can work offline and quickly draw cluster boundaries and rapid random household selection. Download maps for offline use and integrate with ODK Collect or Kobo Toolbox for longer surveys. Strong data encryption and offline summary statistics with pre-programmed analyses.
Training required and manageable learning curve.
Immunization surveillance and measles and rubella. Underimmunized U5 children enumeration. Counts of children U5/mothers living in household.
Nigeria
Free app, training and mapper transportation cost
Surveillance, governance oversight, anlaytics
Satellite Imagery Analysis
Description
Cannot identify people directly, but cars and livestock are visible

Can identify conflict damage to health infrastructure, recent examples include Gaza, Sudan, Syria, Ukraine, and find evidence of settlement habitation changesecent examples include Borno State, Nigeria and Pakistan. Manually assessed data can be turned into training data for machine learning model
Manual or automated processes to observe recent changes visible in satellite imagery, USG provides unparalleled access to unclassified commercial satellite imagery w/ 30-50cm resolution.

Can identify health infrastructure in post-conflict state, establish evidence of settlements for microplanning. Imagery analysis can assess damage to health facilities/hospitals, inhabitance before, during, and after rainy season, among others.
Can be scaled to provide baseline habitation assessments in border areas,
Surveillance, governance oversight, network
Routine immunization among nomadic populations

GIS-based household serology survey used to quantify polio immunity across selected districts using random spatial sampling. Supports identification of immunity gaps not visible from health facility–based sampling. Done in three districts in Afghanistan's Nangarhar and Kandahar provinces. Previously, all polio seroprevalence surveys were conducted at health facility level.
% of expected vs. completed geotagged surveys % surveys in correct clusters % households within 60m of start point
Geospatial analysis of nomadic travel routes to identify high-traffic destinations for targeted vaccination deployment across the Gedo region, Somalia.
Polio, RI
Operational
Operational

Mapping and modeling suitable nomadic camp locations using environmental and accessibility covariates such as distance to water sources, roads, elevation, borehole density, green pastures, and hamlet density. These factors are processed into a suitability surface to predict likely camp sites for outreach. Subsequent steps involve validating these predictions and refining them through human review, then comparing outreach session data across months with hamlet locations and zero-dose child reports to identify geographic gaps.

Integrating nomads into microplans; modeling nomadic locations and mapping movements. This gap analysis enables prioritization of areas requiring additional outreach and informs the placement of temporary vaccination posts along seasonal movement corridors, ensuring comprehensive coverage of hard-to-reach nomadic communities.
Scalabale in countries with seasonal and ad migratory movements across countries with fluid borders.
# of new nomadic camps identified
Surveillance, OBR, analytics
Identify formal and informal border crossing ; This response was conducted in person after Malawi declared a new WPV1 case after 30 years of no detection.
% of crossings with deployed teams Vaccinations delivered at mapped points

Used in pre-campaign preparedness and intra-campaign monitoring. Informal border crossings between the two countries were mapped to deploy vaccination teams strategically. Validation of participatory mapping outputs with satellite imagery. Highly traffic mud paths/ roads seen in imagery with settlements on each side of the border were indicative of border crossing and were adjusted accordingly.
Strategic border vaccination placement to stop cross-border virus movement
Countries having synchronized SIAs, countries with fluid borders.
Surveillance, OBR, governance & oversight
Malawi, Mozambique, Zambia, Tanzania
Research
GIS‑generated random starting points GPS tablet navigation to households Geotagged survey submissions Protocol adherence monitoring
Identifies immunity gaps to guide targeted vaccination and SIA planning.
High (survey design replicable in other settings)
Hybrid

Mendes A, Whiteman A, Nygren B, et al. Immunity to poliovirus in Afghanistan: A household sampling method for serological assessment based on geographical information systems. Geospat Health. 2022;17(2):10.4081/gh.2022.1107. Published 2022 Nov 29. doi:10.4081/gh.2022.1107

Davlantes E. Notes from the Field: Initial Outbreak Response Activity Following Wild Poliovirus Type 1 Detection — Malawi, February 2022. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep 2022;71:776–777. DOI: http://dx.doi.org/10.15585/mmwr.mm7123a3.
Mapping nomadic travel destinations for strategic vaccination deployment. Identify popular travel destinations among nomadic populations to deploy targeted mobile vaccination.
High across pastoralist regions with nomadic populations Can be expanded to Ethiopia, Kenya corridor
Cross-border planning, informs deployment of mobile vaccination teams along cross‑border nomadic routes.
Accuracy of predicted movement hubs (validated vs. field data) Number of targeted vaccination deployments informed by analysis % increase in nomadic children reached
Automated AFP surveillance in tertiary care hospitals using ICD-10 coding

Health information system can automatically flag AFP cases when entered by treating physcian. AFP is a syndrome with a case definition that corresponds with multiple ICD-10 codes. Onces one or more of the codes are entered, the system can flag the case to the central-level surveillance focal person.
Eligible for secondary or tertiary level hospitals using standardized health information systems based on ICD-10 codes.
CDC National Syndromic Syrveillance Program
RI
USA
Missed AFP reporting
Anticipated: government buy-in
Automatically flag AFP cases and avoid missed reporting from traditional paper-based forms.
Can be replicated to influenza, SARS, cholera, yellow fevel, viral hemorrhagic fever.
#system detections not reported by paper-based reporting

Solutions-by challenge
Compenent
Value
Inter-agency Referral Network + migration data: Inter-agency health health/protection referral pathwards, standard practicte in humanitarian operations.

r.t: Use DTM (IOM) + HDX APIs to access non‑sensitive, regularly updated IDP/returnee data at Admin1/Admin2; 91 active country operations.

Inter‑agency referral pathways are supported by UNHCR minimum standards and OCHA coordination guidance—implemented widely, including child protection referral mapping. Sectors included: education, health, shelter, WASH, food security.

si.t: High scalability across crises. Countries with IDPs, host countries to refeguee/asylum seekers, response to irregular migrations.

r.t: Establishing partnerships and referral networks can address missing data retrieved from humanitarian partners can be directly incorporated in in-country

validity in routine polio surveillance. This model can be applied to both countries experiencing conflict or receiving displaced populations. While open source platforns such as Displacement Tracking Matrix and Humanitarian Data Exchange are publicly available, GPEI currently does not have unifrom access to real-time, province and border-level in children U5 eligible for immunization.

si.t: Ground truthing mobility patterns (rapid household travel/vaccination access survey)

Validate mobile population movement, access to services across/after journey. Complement existing data collected by UN OCHA on mobility patterns by deploying surveys on household travel in the last 9 months.

1) Interagency referral system with in-country humanitarian focal points mapped to OCHA/UMHCR referral pathway, 2) Sharing data across humanitarian organizations to identify U5 populations in H2R areas
Operational (global, 91 active country operations)
Deoploy app-based rapid enumeratur surveys with GPS/photo validation to measure household travel in last 9 months, to and from Afghanisn, along with quesitions on vaccine access and hesitancy

CDC-led mapping triangulating multiple outreach data (2016-2020). High-resolution imagery to assess habitation and destruction patterns to classify settlements (inhabited/partially abandoned/abandoned). Objective is to update population estimates, and overlay vaccinator GPS traces to quantify unreached U5 and adjust microplans.
Remote settlers, inaccessible settlements
Geospatial Tracking System: Satellite imagery analysis w/ vaccinator GPS tracking to identify settlement habitation & track vaccination progress

Digital (satellite analytics + GPS telemetry)

Strngthens intra-campaign polio OBR monitoring, outreach immunization services in Egypt, and other ddata collection activities in humanitarian settings. Can collect data offline, used mixed methods surveys, and generate automatic reports of survey results. Access can be managed by more than one user. Supports various data types (text, images, and GPS coordinates), admin credentials can be tranferred and automated reports instantly generated within app.
Digital standardization of campaign forms using open-access tool-KoboToolbox and KoboCollect.

Can be adapted to polio eradication efforts by creating specific surveys for polio surveillance, vaccination coverage, and outbreak response-include synchronized SIAs.

%populations surveyed that have travelled in last 9 months #of travel destinations identified

Passive GPS tracking device inside vaccine carrier, improved verification of vaccinator movement. No interaction with vaccine buddy is needed.

r.t: Identifies inhabited/uninhabited settlements combining satellite imagery, modeled settlement data, and population estimates with vaccinator GPS tracking to create a automated, unified view of both population distribution, settlement extents, building footprints, and campaign reach.
to compare movement against microplans and satellite imagery, and (B)
passive GPS loggers in vaccine carriers (Vaccine Buddy)
capturing location, temperature, and lid events to verify coverage and cold-chain integrity.

Assessed 11,927 settlements (Dec 2017): 8,062 inhabited, 662 partially abandoned, 3,203 abandoned (1,892 destroyed); outputs informed population re‑estimation and unreached‑child targeting. Effective tracking in insecure areas.

si.t: Automatically collects: geolocation, temperature, lid opens/close. Long battery life, ruggedized, water‑resistant, compatible with GTS/ODK/custom systems; supports passive mapping of team tracks; improves tracking where phones are not feasible.
MoH buy-in, import needed
High potential, especially in insecure geographies; integrates with CDC’s GTS and passive GPS strategies
Campaign geographic coverage (expected vs. actual) % planned route completed, GPS points logged # lid‑open events, device uptime
DRC-pilot, Nigeria (Borno)
Planfeld

https://cdc.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/teams/CGH-GID-PEB/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B70F0F586-A39B-471D-A47A-22C7EF8B7237%7D&file=19_Using%20GIS%20Application%20for%20SIA%20Microplanning%20%26%20PEI%20activities.pptx&action=edit&mobileredirect=true&DefaultItemOpen=1
Urban, multi‑floor households with poor vertical access.
Identifies high‑rise building clusters in districts repeatedly failing LQAS; maps approximate floor counts and building footprint clusters to guide team routing.
Can be scaled to other vaccines. High risk setting to verify vaccinator movement.

Reduces microplan denominator errors; optimizes team‑per‑building allocation. Scaled in microplanning and post-campaign monitoring by correctly identifying number and floor of urban inhabitants, thereby minimizing loss in reaching target population.
# additional units identified per floor LQAS pass rate improvement % mapped buildings included in microplans
Remote settlers, disperserd rural households

Combines GPS‑tagged house‑to‑house enumeration, satellite imagery, and team tracking to map settlements, facilities, boundaries, and children 0–23 months with vaccination status. Mapping, enumeration, microplanning, validation, team tracking, data integration-field staff combined with satellite imagery.

Engage local staff to map settlements, facilities, and boundaries using GPS and satellite imagery. Specific steps: 1. Enumeration: GPS-tag house-to-house data on children (0–23 months), vaccination status, and non-vaccination reasons. 2. Microplanning: Use maps showing never-vaccinated rates, main reasons, and facility lCombines GPS‑tagged house‑to‑house enumeration, satellite imagery, and team tracking to map settlements, facilities, boundaries, and children 0–23 months with vaccination status. 3. Validation: Clean and verify spatial data with local input, GRID3, and satellite imagery. 4. Team Tracking: GPS-track vaccination teams to confirm locality visits and coverage of remote areas. 5. Data Integration: Link enumeration with boundaries to estimate missed children and visualize coverage post-campaign.
CDC GRASP Team, local DRC field teams
Moderate — methods replicable, depends on funding, field teams + imagery;
# housing units mapped # missed children identified Zone‑level denominator changes
One‑stop tool for rapid cluster boundary drawing, random HH selection, offline map download, and in‑field navigation; integrates with ODK/KoBo for longer surveys.
CDC GRASP Team, DGHP, Georgia Tech

Enhances LQAS & serosurvey sampling, enabling random household selection and navigation in one field visit. Immunization surveillance and measles and rubella. Underimmunized U5 children enumeration. Counts of children U5/mothers living in household.

Although often differing from the actual political boundaries of a given settlement on the ground, they offer a way to rapidly LOCATE, IDENTIFY, and QUANTIFY population in the most remote areas where traditional maps fall short. They can be used to rapidly triage and target areas for programmatic work by estimated population size, distance from resources, and other geographic factors. Produces encrypted data; offline statistics; speeds sampling; reduces enumerator error; supports cluster‑level U5 counts.
Training needed; transportation costs; manageable learning curve.
Free app
# clusters sampled Sampling error rate Enumerator completion time GPS accuracy
https://www.gpssample.org/home
Hybrid, can be done remole if one or more field staff already trained
GRID3, CDC GRASP, local country partners.
Remote settlers, scattered rural communities

Provides global, uniform building footprints & settlement extents, enabling rapid identification of previously unmapped remote populations. GRID3 has algorithmically combined these footprints into “settlements” with GIS boundaries.Big data powerhouses like Google, Meta, Microsoft, and others have used massive computing resources to automatically scan satellite imagery and identify likely human-made structures.

Accelerates mapping & prioritization of inaccessible communities, assist with population enumeration where census data are weak or outdated. Automatically generated settlement extents and building footprints provide a fast, reliable way to locate and quantify populations living in remote or poorly mapped areas.

Datasets produced globally, allowing polio programs to triage settlements by estimated population size, accessibility, and distance from essential resources, and to apply the same data-driven approach across multiple geographies, including cross-border transmission zones.
# settlements identified % newly mapped areas integrated into microplans
Remote settlers, conflict‑affected populations

Identifies post‑conflict damage, non‑functioning facilities, seasonal habitation; improves microplanning and border‑area SIA design. Can identify conflict damage to health infrastructure and find evidence of settlement habitation changes. Manually assessed data can be turned into training data for machine learning model
Cannot identify people directly, but cars and livestock are visible. Needs ground truthing.
Satellite imagery free. Cost associated with ground-truthing, if implemented.
High replicability, satellite images covers global scope.
# non‑functioning facilities # inhabited settlements detected # seasonal habitation change patterns
https://grid3.org/
Summary of Selected Solutions
Strategic Solution
Selection Criteria
- Proven effectiveness in field setting
- Can be designed and implemented by CDC
- Non-obstructive to existing GPEI processes
- Cost-effective
- Scalable
- Time-limite
- Aligns with existing policies:
- WHO withdrawal
- GPEI transition plan
- OneCDC approach
- GID Priority Pillars
- CDC Global Health Security Agenda & International Health Regulations

r.t: Intervention that can digital or non-digital that can be directly and pre-emptively applied to a GPEI process to tackle a documented challenge.

si.t: Campaign Effectiveness
PlanFeld Digital Microplanning & Campaign Monitoring System

Web-based GIS microplanning and monitoring system that automates daily team plans, uses updated settlement master lists, validates microplans, and integrates GPS visitation tracking and GIS-based monitoring. Presentation highlights that PlanFeld digitizes microplanning, reduces time, improves accuracy of team area boundaries, and enhances cost-effectiveness (Slide 3, 28).

It is used to digitize microplans for Polio SIAs, ensuring validated settlement master lists and accurate geo-coordinates support implementation. Supports supervisors to deploy to unvisited settlements using daily GIS feedback (Slides 35, 55).

High – improves microplan accuracy, reduces missed settlements, ensures validated settlement lists, supports proper team demarcation, guides mop-up planning with GTS/ODK overlays (Slides 22–24, 34, 41, 55).
Remote, urban, migratory, conflict-affected (applicable anywhere geodatabase exists)
Humanitarian & health campaigns (Polio SIAs, OBR, fixed-post campaigns)
Nigeria (national PEI program), scalable to other countries
eHealth Africa (eHA)

Automates microplanning; updates team boundaries; integrates GPS-based tracking; supports geospatial validation of settlement lists; aligns team maps with GTS and ODK supervision data; improves identification of missed settlements; enhances navigation through clearer boundaries and landmarks (Slides 23–27, 28, 41, 55).
Adequate field devices required; GTS/ODK data quality depends on proper tracker use; requires validated settlement lists; internet limitations in insecure or remote areas (Slides 35, 55).
Not specified (digital platform; likely license/subscription).
Very high – used at scale in Nigeria; reduces planning time; supports multi-day daily implementation plans and mop-up prioritization; improves LGA-level decision-making (Slides 27, 34, 35).

% settlements visited vs planned; # missed settlements identified by GTS; % team tracks aligned with assigned boundaries; # microplan corrections after GIS validation; time to generate plans; supervision coverage gaps identified (Slides 34, 36, 41, 55).
Surveillance; OBR; governance & oversight; analytics; network

Nomadic, pastoralist, highly ,mobile groups
Quality of predictive data, limited access for validation, insecurity; mitigated through local outreach teams, community checkpoints, and field verification
# new nomadic camps identified # outreach sessions in newly mapped areas ZD children reported Comparison of modeled vs. verified locations

Identifies immunity gaps to guide targeted vaccination and SIA planning. Quantifies true population immunity, identifies immunity gaps not visible through health‑facility sampling, and guides microplanning for SIAs in high‑risk reservoirs.
Remote settlers, peri-urban

Geotagged sampling verifies protocol compliance; allows identification of clusters of low seropositivity; contrasts household immunity with health‑facility–based estimates; improves understanding of geographically heterogeneous protection in persistent reservoirs: GIS‑generated random starting points GPS tablet navigation to households Geotagged survey submissions Protocol adherence monitoring

Migratory, border populations

Participatory mapping exercises with field teams were combined with high‑resolution satellite imagery to identify formal and informal crossing points following the Malawi WPV1 detection. Field teams validated local reports of footpaths, bicycle trails, and riverbank approaches, and aligned these with imagery‑visible tracks and settlement clusters. The outputs were then used to pre‑position vaccination teams at high‑traffic border points.
Cost for groundtruthing and staff per diems
Hybrid, imagery analysis and map creation remotely; ground‑truthing in country.

Movement patterns of nomads identified in Gedo region. Destinations identified: Dolo, Luuq, Beletawa, Garbahare, El Waq, Bardera, Afmadow, Kismayo, Balad, Mogadishu. Nomadic mobility in the Gedo region is highly interconnected, with strong internal movement and several dominant destination hubs—particularly Afmadow and northern Gedo districts such as Luuq and Dolo. The network suggests both localized circulation and broader regional connectivity, with certain districts functioning as key population aggregation points.

Locations may not be precise to a deployable location. Nomadic paths shift with rainfall, pasture, and security. Reliance on community‑reported routes introduces recall bias. Mitigation included triangulation using environmental suitability layers, historical movement patterns, and cross‑checking with CHW and NGO field logs.
Low starting cost with limited verification but increases with new field deployments to new;y predicted locations.

Cross-border planning, informs deployment of mobile vaccination teams along cross‑border nomadic routes.Identifies seasonal congregation points for targetted SIA action. Ensures vaccination team track seasonal movement rather than fixed settlements.
Transit Point Vaccination (TPV) Model
Migratory, conflict-affected, border populations

Temorary or semi-permanent vaccination stations at informal border crossings/natural transit notes. Teams screen and vaccinate children on the move, regardless of origin or documentation, focusing on zero‑dose and under‑immunized groups.
# children screened/vaccinated % ZD geographic flows tracked
Can capture children who would not be reached through fixed facilities due to distance, insecurity, or lack of services. High relevance for cross-border polio transmission corridors.
No, vaccination requires field presence, planning and data analysis can be done remotely.
Populations seeking facility-based care (tertiary)

Health information system can automatically flag AFP cases when entered by treating physcian. AFP is a syndrome with a case definition that corresponds with multiple ICD-10 codes. Onces one or more of the codes are entered, the system can flag the case to the central-level surveillance focal person, eliminating dependence on manual reporting channels.

Eligiblilty: secondary or tertiary level hospitals using standardized health information systems based on ICD-10 codes. Automatically flags AFP cases that might otherwise be missed due to incomplete paper forms or delayed clinician reporting. Ensures real‑time notifications sent to surveillance focal persons. Ensures early detection of acute flaccid paralysis among hospitalized children.
Government buy-in
Scalable to countries with electronic system using ICD‑10‑aligned digital HMIS, can already experience poliovirus circulation or a neighbouring country.
Can be replicated to influenza, SARS, cholera, yellow fevel, viral hemorrhagic fever. Sustainability depends on continued use of standardized coding and IT system upkeep.
Number of system‑flagged AFP detections Number of system alerts not reported through paper forms Timeliness of notification from code entry to central‑level alert
Populations with varying level of trust in vaccines

Rapid community surveys administered by the Premise digital network assess perceptions related to vaccine safety, side effects, rumors, and general trust. Data are collected before campaigns to identify emerging narratives and geographical clusters of hesitancy. (Tanzania, 2023)
# of community member surveyed # of misconceptions identified Comparative rates of vaccince confidence across time and geographies
Internally displaced families, mobile households, or low routine immunization contact
Operational (GAVI ZD Project)
Cameroon (Kumba North & Kumba South districts)

CHWs visted 9,450 homes and identified and vaccinated 3,172 ZD children (Penta-1) and 2,893 Penta-3; engaged 9,450+ homes. Targetted geographies have high numbers of internally displaced populations and weakened health system post-COVD pandemic.
